section.title: Job Replies to Bildad
section.sectionRationale: Job does not dispute that God is powerful and just in the abstract; he agrees entirely, and that is precisely his problem. If God is this vast and this sovereign, no mortal stands a chance in court against Him, and worse, there is no mediator to stand between them. This speech contains the book's aching cry for an arbiter, a longing that will not be answered until chapter 19 and, ultimately, until the New Testament names who that mediator is.
section.thesis: Job agrees God is overwhelmingly powerful and just, but concludes this makes it impossible for a mortal to contend with Him directly, and cries out for a mediator who could lay his hand on them both, a longing the book leaves unanswered here but will not leave unanswered forever.
section.frameNote: Six movements: God's incomparable power over creation and the impossibility of answering Him; Job's admission he could only beg for mercy, since God seems to destroy the blameless and the wicked alike; Job's longing for a mediator, since none exists between him and God; his direct plea that God stop condemning him without explanation; his meditation on being shaped by God's own hands, now seemingly turned to destroy him; and his closing lament asking why he was ever born from the womb at all.

units.label: How can a mortal be righteous before God?
units.note: the Maker of the Bear and Orion

verses.text: Then Job answered:
"Yes, I know that it is so, but how can a mortal be righteous before God?
If one wished to contend with God, he could not answer Him one time out of a thousand.
God is wise in heart and mighty in strength. Who has resisted Him and prospered?
He moves mountains without their knowledge and overturns them in His anger.
He shakes the earth from its place, so that its foundations tremble.
He commands the sun not to shine; He seals off the stars.
He alone stretches out the heavens and treads on the waves of the sea.
He is the Maker of the Bear and Orion, of the Pleiades and the constellations of the south.
He does great things beyond searching out, and wonders without number.
Were He to pass by me, I would not see Him; were He to move, I would not recognize Him.
If He takes away, who can stop Him? Who dares to ask Him, 'What are You doing?'
God does not restrain His anger; the helpers of {{rahab|Rahab}} cower beneath Him.

units.label: I could only beg my Judge for mercy
units.note: He destroys both the blameless and the wicked

verses.text: "How then can I answer Him or choose my arguments against Him?
For even if I were right, I could not answer. I could only beg my Judge for mercy.
If I summoned Him and He answered me, I do not believe He would listen to my voice.
For He would crush me with a tempest and multiply my wounds {{chinam|without cause}}.
He does not let me catch my breath, but overwhelms me with bitterness.
If it is a matter of strength, He is indeed mighty! If it is a matter of justice, who can summon Him?
Even if I were righteous, my mouth would condemn me; if I were {{tam|blameless}}, it would declare me guilty.
Though I am blameless, I have no concern for myself; I despise my own life.
It is all the same, and so I say, 'He destroys both the blameless and the wicked.'
When the scourge brings sudden death, He mocks the despair of the innocent.

The earth is given into the hand of the wicked; He blindfolds its judges. 
If it is not He, then who is it?

units.label: Nor is there a mediator between us
units.note: let Him remove His rod from me

verses.text: My days are swifter than a runner; they flee without seeing good.
They sweep by like boats of papyrus, like an eagle swooping down on its prey.
If I were to say, 'I will forget my complaint and change my expression and smile,'
I would still dread all my sufferings; I know that You will not acquit me.
Since I am already found guilty, why should I labor in vain?
If I should wash myself with snow and cleanse my hands with lye,
then You would plunge me into the pit, and even my own clothes would despise me.
For He is not a man like me, that I can answer Him, that we can take each other to court.
Nor is there a {{yakach|mediator}} between us, to lay his hand upon us both.
Let Him remove His rod from me, so that His terror will no longer frighten me.
Then I would speak without fear of Him. But as it is, I am on my own.

units.label: Do not condemn me
units.note: let me know why You prosecute me

verses.text:
"I loathe my own life; I will express my complaint 
and speak in the bitterness of my soul.

I will say to God: Do not condemn me! 
Let me know why You prosecute me.

Does it please You to oppress me, to reject the work of Your hands 
and favor the schemes of the wicked?
Do You have eyes of flesh? Do You see as man sees?
Are Your days like those of a mortal, or Your years like those of a man,
that You should seek my iniquity and search out my sin,
though You know that I am not guilty, and there is no deliverance from Your hand?

units.label: Your hands shaped me
units.note: would You now return me to dust

verses.text: "Your hands shaped me and altogether formed me. Would You now turn and destroy me?
Please remember that You molded me like clay. Would You now return me to dust?
Did You not pour me out like milk, and curdle me like cheese?
You clothed me with skin and flesh, and knit me together with bones and sinews.
You have granted me life and loving devotion, and Your care has preserved my spirit.
Yet You concealed these things in Your heart, and I know that this was in Your mind:
If I sinned, You would take note, and would not acquit me of my iniquity.
If I am guilty, woe to me! And even if I am righteous, I cannot lift my head. I am full of shame and aware of my affliction.

Should I hold my head high, You would hunt me like a lion, 
and again display Your power against me.
You produce new witnesses against me and multiply Your anger toward me. Hardships assault me in wave after wave.

units.label: Why did You bring me from the womb?
units.note: a land of darkness and gloom

verses.text: Why then did You bring me from the womb? Oh, that I had died, and no eye had seen me!
If only I had never come to be, but had been carried from the womb to the grave.
Are my days not few? Withdraw from me, that I may have a little comfort,
before I go, never to return, to a land of darkness and gloom,

to a land of utter darkness, of deep shadow and disorder, 
where even the light is like darkness."

rahab.gloss: A mythic sea monster or chaos figure, distinct from the woman Rahab of Jericho though spelled similarly in transliteration. Job pictures even Rahab's helpers, the forces of primeval chaos, cowering beneath God's power, an image of overwhelming, uncontestable strength.

chinam.gloss: Without cause, for nothing, the same word God used of Job's suffering in 2:3. Here Job turns it back on God Himself, accusing Him of multiplying wounds without cause, showing that Job has heard and internalized the very truth the reader already knows, even as he cannot reconcile it with a God he still believes is just.

tam.gloss: Blameless, complete, the same word used of Job by the narrator and by God Himself in chapters 1 and 2. Job insists he is still tam even as he despairs, showing that his self-assessment has not wavered even though his circumstances have made that claim seem, to him, almost worthless to make.

yakach.gloss: To arbitrate, decide, or mediate between two parties. Job's cry that there is no mediator, one who could lay his hand on both God and Job, names the exact lack the whole book will leave unresolved until Job's later hope in a Redeemer, and that only the New Testament finally answers in the person of Christ, 1 Timothy 2:5.

basic.context: Job agrees with everything Bildad has said about God's power and justice in the abstract; his problem is precisely that agreement. If God moves mountains, seals the stars, and treads on the sea itself, no mortal can possibly win a case against Him, even if that mortal is innocent. Job pictures himself hopelessly outmatched in any court, unable even to be heard, and cries out for someone who could stand between himself and God as a mediator, laying a hand on them both. He then turns to address God directly, asking why the very hands that shaped him in the womb now seem turned against him, and closes wishing he had never been born at all.
basic.hermeneutics: This speech is the theological center of gravity for the whole book's ache: not a denial of God's power or justice, but a recognition that power and justice alone cannot bridge the gap between an infinite, righteous God and a finite, suffering creature without an arbiter. Job's cry for a mediator in 9:33 is not answered in this chapter; it will not be answered until chapter 19's flash of hope in a living Redeemer, and finally not until Christ, who alone can lay a hand on both God and humanity.
basic.originalLanguages: Job pictures even the helpers of rahab, the mythic chaos monster, cowering before God's unrestrained power, and he throws God's own word, chinam, without cause, back at Him, accusing Him of multiplying wounds for no reason, the same word God used to describe Job's suffering to Satan. Job insists he remains tam, blameless, even in despair, and voices the book's aching central longing for a yakach, a mediator, who does not yet exist for him.
basic.typology: Job's cry for a mediator who could lay a hand on both parties directly anticipates the office Christ alone fulfills, the one mediator between God and mankind, Himself both God and man. His complaint that God's hands both formed him and now seem to destroy him anticipates the deeper mystery of a God who both judges sin and, in Christ, bears its penalty Himself.
basic.christ: Job's unanswered longing, nor is there a mediator between us, to lay his hand upon us both, is the clearest prophetic ache in this section of the book. Paul will later name exactly this office filled: there is one God and one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus. Job could only imagine such a figure; the gospel announces that He has come, able to lay one hand on God as truly divine and one hand on humanity as truly human, closing the very gap Job despaired of ever seeing bridged.
basic.god: God is presented here in overwhelming, even terrifying, sovereign power, moving mountains and sealing stars, a picture Job does not dispute but finds crushing rather than comforting given his circumstances. The chapter does not soften God's power to make Job's complaint easier; it lets both truths stand together, God's incomparable might and Job's honest terror before it, without resolving the tension.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Timothy 2:5 names the one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus, directly answering Job's lament in 9:33; Hebrews 4:15-16 promises a high priest who sympathizes with weakness and invites bold approach to the throne of grace; Romans 8:33-34 answers Job's fear of condemnation with the assurance that no charge can stand against those in Christ.
basic.oneStory: Job's cry for a mediator is the book's deepest unanswered ache, a longing for someone able to stand between a righteous, overwhelming God and a suffering, finite creature. The whole biblical story moves toward answering that exact cry in Jesus Christ, the one mediator who lays His hand on both, closing the gap Job could only imagine.

context.literary: Job's reply spans two chapters, moving from cosmic praise of God's power, through legal imagery of an impossible lawsuit, to the aching cry for a mediator, and finally into direct address of God that mirrors and intensifies his shift in 7:11.
context.historical: The legal imagery, contending, answering, being acquitted, reflects ancient Near Eastern court procedure, where a wronged party could seek an arbiter or advocate; Job's complaint that no such figure exists for him against God highlights the uniqueness of his predicament.
context.canonical: Job 9:33's cry for a mediator stands as one of the Old Testament's most direct expressions of the need later fulfilled in Christ's mediatorial office, a thread that runs from here through Job's further hope in chapter 19 to its full revelation in the New Testament.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To voice, with theological precision, the impossibility of a finite, even innocent, creature successfully contending with an infinite, sovereign God without some mediating figure, preparing the reader for both Job's later flashes of hope and the New Testament's full answer.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Job's despairing legal reasoning and his longing for a mediator; it does not prescribe that God is capricious or unjust, a conclusion Job himself does not fully draw even here. What endures as abiding truth is the genuine impossibility, apart from a mediator, of any sinner successfully standing before God's perfect justice on their own merits.

debates.question: Does Job's statement that God 'destroys both the blameless and the wicked' (9:22) accuse God of injustice?

views.label: A raw but not ultimately blasphemous observation about apparent outcomes
views.case: Job is describing what he observes in the world, not making a settled doctrinal claim; his own continued appeal to God as Judge, verse 15, shows he has not actually abandoned belief in God's justice.

views.label: A genuinely dangerous overstatement that edges toward accusing God of moral indifference
views.case: This kind of language is part of what God will later confront in the whirlwind speeches, correcting Job for words that obscured counsel without knowledge, suggesting this statement goes further than simple lament.

debates.bearing: The tension here is honest and unresolved within the chapter itself; readers should neither dismiss Job's words as harmless nor rush to condemn a suffering man's grasping attempt to make sense of what he sees.

study.originalLanguages: Job pictures the helpers of rahab, the chaos monster, cowering before God, and turns God's own word, chinam, without cause, back on Him as an accusation. He insists he remains tam, blameless, even in despair, and voices the book's central unanswered longing for a yakach, a mediator, capable of laying one hand on God and one hand on Job, a role only the New Testament will finally reveal as fulfilled in Christ.

typology.type: The absence of a mediator able to lay a hand on both God and Job, 9:33
typology.fulfillment: 1 Timothy 2:5, the one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus, both fully God and fully human.

typology.type: Job's complaint that the hands that formed him now seem to destroy him
typology.fulfillment: The deeper mystery of the cross, where the God who judges sin also bears its judgment Himself in Christ.

study.christ: Job's aching cry, nor is there a mediator between us, names precisely the office the New Testament will announce as filled: one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus, who is Himself both fully God and fully human and so can lay one hand on each. Job could only imagine such a figure and despair of finding one; the gospel announces His arrival, closing the very gap that made Job's case before God feel so hopeless.
study.god: God is shown here in overwhelming, cosmic power, moving mountains, sealing the stars, treading on the sea, might Job does not dispute but experiences as crushing given his circumstances. The chapter refuses to soften this picture of divine sovereignty in order to make Job's suffering easier to bear, letting both realities, God's incomparable greatness and Job's honest terror, stand together without resolution.

crossReferences.note: There is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus, directly answering Job's lament that no such figure exists for him in 9:33.
A high priest who sympathizes with our weakness invites bold approach to the throne of grace, the answer to Job's fear of being unable to approach God at all.
No charge can stand against those in Christ, since He intercedes for them, answering Job's dread that God would not acquit him even if he were innocent.

study.oneStory: Job agrees God is powerful and just, and precisely because of that agreement, he despairs of ever successfully contending with Him without a mediator. That unanswered longing, voiced here in the book's theological center of gravity, drives the whole of Scripture forward to its answer: Christ, the one mediator who lays His hand on both God and humanity and closes the gap Job could only grieve over.

quiz.q: Why does Job say a mortal cannot be righteous before God (9:2-3)?

options.text: Because God's power and wisdom are so overwhelming that a mortal could not answer Him even one time out of a thousand.
options.note: Job's whole argument in this section builds on the sheer scale of God's power described in the following verses.

options.text: Because God moves mountains and shakes the earth, a power far beyond anything a mortal could resist or contend with.
options.note: A true supporting detail from the passage, though less precise than Job's direct statement about the impossibility of answering God even once in a thousand times.

options.text: Because his friends have finally convinced him of his guilt.
options.note: Job explicitly rejects his friends' arguments; his despair here is about the nature of standing before God, not agreement with the friends.

options.text: Because Job believes he has actually committed some specific hidden sin.
options.note: Job maintains his innocence throughout; his point is about the impossibility of contending with God, not a confession of guilt.

quiz.q: What does Job long for in 9:33 that does not yet exist for him?

options.text: A mediator who could lay his hand on both God and himself.
options.note: This is the exact wording of Job's lament, a longing the New Testament will later answer in Christ, 1 Timothy 2:5.

options.text: Someone able to arbitrate his case fairly before God, since he is not a man like God whom he could simply answer.
options.note: Captures the substance of Job's longing, though less precise than the exact image of a mediator laying a hand on both parties.

options.text: A written record of his good deeds.
options.note: Job does long to be heard and vindicated, which will surface later in chapter 19, but the specific longing named in 9:33 is for a mediator, not a written record.

options.text: A larger inheritance for his descendants.
options.note: Job's longing here is specifically for an arbiter between himself and God, not for material inheritance.

quiz.q: What troubling claim does Job make in 9:22, and how should it be read?

options.text: That God destroys both the blameless and the wicked alike; it is an honest but raw observation from a man in anguish, not a settled doctrinal statement.
options.note: The chapter presents this as Job's grieving attempt to make sense of what he observes, within a speech that still appeals to God as Judge.

options.text: That outcomes in this life do not reliably distinguish between the righteous and the wicked.
options.note: A true summary of Job's underlying observation, though less pointed than his own raw wording that God destroys both alike.

options.text: That Job has proof of a specific instance where God acted wrongly.
options.note: Job offers this as a general, pained observation rather than a specific proven case.

options.text: That God has abandoned the world to chaos with no ongoing involvement.
options.note: Job's whole speech assumes God's active, powerful involvement in the world; his complaint is that this involvement feels unjust, not absent.

quiz.q: In chapter 10, what does Job appeal to about his own origin?

options.text: That God's own hands shaped and formed him in the womb, making it seem contradictory that those same hands would now seem turned to destroy him.
options.note: Verses 8-12 develop this meditation on divine craftsmanship now apparently working against its own creation.

options.text: That God clothed him with skin and flesh and granted him life, care that now seems reversed in his present affliction.
options.note: A true detail from Job's meditation, though less complete than the full contrast between careful creation and apparent destruction.

options.text: That his birth was entirely random and outside God's involvement.
options.note: Job's whole point is the opposite: God was intimately, deliberately involved in forming him, which is what makes his current suffering feel so contradictory.

options.text: That his parents were responsible for his current suffering.
options.note: Job's appeal in this chapter is to God as his maker, not to any human parent.

quiz.q: How does this speech's cry for a mediator connect to the rest of Scripture?

options.text: It anticipates 1 Timothy 2:5's declaration that there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus, who can lay a hand on both parties as Job longed for.
options.note: This connection is one of the clearest typological threads running from Job through to the New Testament's doctrine of Christ's mediatorial office.

options.text: It points forward to a figure who could represent humanity fully before God, later revealed more clearly in the New Testament.
options.note: Correct in its general direction, though less precise than naming 1 Timothy 2:5's specific answer of Christ as the one mediator.

options.text: It is fulfilled by the priesthood of Aaron established later in Exodus.
options.note: The Aaronic priesthood offers partial mediation through sacrifice, but Job's specific longing, one who can lay a hand on both God and man as equal parties, is uniquely fulfilled in the God-man, Christ.

options.text: It has no meaningful connection to any later biblical teaching.
options.note: The New Testament's teaching on Christ's mediatorial role directly answers this exact longing voiced in Job 9:33.

questions: Job agrees God is powerful and just, and that very agreement deepens his despair rather than resolving it. Has believing something true about God ever felt harder to bear than comforting, and how did you sit with that tension?
Job cries out for a mediator who does not yet exist for him. How does knowing that Christ is exactly that mediator change the way you bring your own hardest complaints before God?

Job accuses God of multiplying his wounds without cause, using God's own word back against Him. Have you ever found yourself arguing with God using truths you actually believe, and what did that reveal about your faith?

Job meditates on being formed by God's own hands, now seemingly turned against him. Where do you find it hard to reconcile God's intimate care in shaping your life with the pain you are currently experiencing?

This speech ends without resolution, in a longing for darkness and rest. What helps you keep bringing unresolved pain back to God in prayer, rather than either denying it or giving up on Him entirely?

translationNotes.note: The helpers of Rahab refers to a mythic chaos monster tradition, distinct from the woman Rahab of Jericho; the image conveys forces of untamed chaos submitting entirely to God's power.

Without cause renders chinam, the same word God used in 2:3 to describe Job's undeserved suffering; Job here turns the word back toward God as an accusation, showing he has absorbed the very truth the reader already knows.

Mediator renders yakach, meaning one who arbitrates or decides between two parties; this verse is widely recognized as an Old Testament anticipation of the mediatorial office fulfilled in Christ, 1 Timothy 2:5.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Job 9:1-10:22. The em dashes in verses 10:6 and 10:21 of the source text were replaced with commas per house style; no other substantive changes were made to the verse text.
